ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general seafood soup class (FOODON_03315595); no specific abalone seafood soup entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fish and seafood soups broadly; no abalone-specific soup code exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 600 g, representing a full bowl of premium seafood soup with abalone and two types of fish as typically served at a Singaporean seafood soup restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Soup (Singaporean style, mixed fish and seafood) — adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central entries for fish soup, abalone, and mixed fish components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo); USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for premium abalone seafood soup with two fish types. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for Singaporean fish soup (clear broth style) and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for abalone (NDB 15150) and mixed fish fillet (NDB 15001). Abalone contributes approximately 15–20% of total dish weight; mixed fish fillets contribute approximately 25–30%; broth, tofu, and vegetables make up the remainder. Weighted composite per-100g values are derived accordingly. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fish-based soups and mixed seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fish soup and low-GI seafood dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for premium abalone seafood soup. GI estimated at 32 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for clear fish-based soups and mixed seafood dishes, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 28–40). The low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100g) and high protein and water content of this dish support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 19.2) / 100 = 6.1 (using 600g serving with 3.2g carbohydrate per 100g = 19.2g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish broth and preserved vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as a single 600g serving provides approximately 2,520 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced salt or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 32) and low carbohydrate content (approximately 19g per serving), making it a suitable protein-rich meal option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish offset by high sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mixed fish and abalone in this soup provide beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein that support cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from the broth warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control or low-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Premium Seafood Soup with Abalone and Mixed Fish is a luxurious Singaporean Chinese-style clear broth soup featuring abalone and two types of fish fillet as its centrepiece ingredients. The dish is prepared by simmering a rich fish-based broth with fresh or live abalone, mixed fish fillets (commonly snapper, grouper, or batang), tofu, tomato, preserved vegetables (chai poh), and ginger, resulting in a delicate yet flavourful soup with a clean, umami-rich taste. The texture profile includes tender, slightly chewy abalone, soft flaky fish, and silky tofu in a light, clear broth. This dish is a premium offering in Singapore's seafood soup restaurant scene, typically priced at the higher end of the market and served as a shared centrepiece meal. Nutritionally, it is high in lean protein (approximately 7.8g per 100g), low in fat (1.8g per 100g), and low in carbohydrates (3.2g per 100g), making it a nutrient-dense, low-glycemic meal. The fish components provide omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. Abalone contributes additional micronutrients including iron and zinc. The primary clinical concern is the sodium content from the broth, which can be significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, this dish is well-suited due to its low GI and high protein content. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this specific premium abalone seafood soup dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of HPB Singapore Foodo fish soup entries and USDA FoodData Central data for abalone and mixed fish components. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar fish-based soups. Confidence is medium due to the composite and analogous estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries for this dish type. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in fish and seafood-based soups per USDA SR Legacy data. The dish description from field survey notes that Fish Head is no longer served in this dish; the record reflects mixed fish fillet composition accordingly. Serving size of 600g is an estimate for a premium restaurant portion; actual serving size may vary by restaurant. Sodium content is notably high due to the fish broth base and preserved vegetable components; this is consistent with HPB Foodo data for Singaporean fish soups. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for clear fish-based soups (GI range 28–40); no direct measurement available for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving = (32 × 19.2) / 100 = 6.1 (low GL). FoodOn match type is closest_parent as no abalone-specific seafood soup class exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent as no abalone-specific soup code exists in FoodEx2.
